region_of_origin: Italy (Mediterranean); widely served in Singapore and internationally

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard appetizer plate of calamari fritti weighs approximately 200 g, representing a typical restaurant starter portion of crispy fried squid rings served with aioli and lemon.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, fried (FDC ID 175168)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried squid (FDC ID 175168) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values are consistent with the input record (energy 290 kcal, protein 15 g, fat 20 g, carbohydrate 12.5 g per 100 g) and have been cross-validated against this approved source. Minor micronutrient values (calcium 25 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 175 mg, vitamin C 5 mg) are within the plausible range for fried squid per USDA data. Sodium 425 mg/100 g is slightly elevated relative to USDA baseline (~260 mg) and reflects added salt in the breading and aioli preparation typical of restaurant-style calamari fritti; this is documented as an adaptation. Trans fat 0.25 g/100 g reflects frying oil contribution and is plausible for deep-fried preparations.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for calamari fritti in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on analogous fried battered seafood and fried wheat-coated foods from the University of Sydney GI Database. The wheat-flour and breadcrumb coating is the primary carbohydrate contributor; fried battered fish and similar coated seafood products cluster in the GI 45–55 range. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (50 × 25.0) / 100 = 12.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit calamari fritti to small occasional portions, request reduced salt in the breading, avoid mayonnaise-based aioli (which adds further sodium), and prefer lemon juice as a condiment to minimise sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (12.5 g/100 g, GI ~50, GL 12.5 per 200 g serving) from the wheat-flour and breadcrumb coating; high fat content (20 g/100 g) increases caloric density and may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume calamari fritti in small portions occasionally, pairing it with non-starchy vegetables, avoiding high-sugar dipping sauces, and monitoring post-meal glucose response given the moderate glycemic load and high caloric density from deep-frying.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (20 g/100 g), cholesterol (175 mg/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g from frying oil), and sodium (425 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk; deep-frying also generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat calamari fritti as an occasional indulgence only, preferring grilled or sautéed squid preparations to eliminate trans fats and AGE formation, and strictly limiting portion size to reduce sodium and cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly exacerbate joint inflammation; this is a hard disqualifying factor for rheumatoid arthritis regardless of other nutrient values.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried calamari and instead request grilled or sautéed squid to eliminate AGE formation while retaining the lean protein and omega-3 benefits of squid.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Calamari Fritti (Italian: 'fried squid') is a classic Mediterranean appetiser consisting of squid rings and tentacles coated in seasoned wheat flour or breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish originated in Italian coastal cuisine and is now widely served in restaurants across Singapore and internationally as a starter or snack. The exterior is light and crunchy while the interior squid flesh remains tender and mildly sweet with a characteristic oceanic flavour. It is typically served with a creamy garlic aioli and a fresh lemon wedge. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 290 kcal, 15 g protein, 20 g fat (including 3 g saturated fat and 0.25 g trans fat from frying oil), and 12.5 g carbohydrate from the coating. Sodium is elevated at 425 mg/100 g due to seasoned breading and aioli. Cholesterol is 175 mg/100 g reflecting the squid's natural cholesterol content. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low) based on the wheat-flour coating. Clinically, the high sodium and fat content make this a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease; the deep-frying process generates AGEs that are particularly problematic for rheumatoid arthritis. For Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate glycemic load (GL ~12.5 per 200 g serving) and high caloric density warrant portion control. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found on Italian restaurant menus, Western casual dining menus, and international food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried squid (FDC ID 175168) and have been cross-validated against this approved source. The sodium value (425 mg/100 g) is adapted upward from the USDA baseline to reflect restaurant-style preparation with seasoned breading and aioli. GI is an analogous estimate from fried battered seafood data in the University of Sydney GI Database, not a direct measurement for calamari fritti. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
